introduction.tts_coach: Alright, let's get straight to it. Six sounds today. These unlock actual English words you'll see everywhere - texts, signs, homework, all of it. No fluff. Let's go.
introduction.tts_coach_ssml: <speak version="1.0" xml:lang="en-US" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2001/10/synthesis"><voice name="en-US-JennyNeural">Alright, let's get straight to it.<break time="200ms"/> Six sounds today.<break time="300ms"/> These unlock actual English words you'll see everywhere - texts, signs, homework, all of it.<break time="200ms"/> No fluff.<break time="300ms"/> Let's go.</voice></speak>

alphabet_practice.tts_coach: Letter S makes /sss/. It's all air, no voice. Watch the screen - teeth almost together, tongue stays behind. Listen: /sss/. Say it: /sss/.
alphabet_practice.tts_coach_ssml: <speak version="1.0" xml:lang="en-US" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2001/10/synthesis"><voice name="en-US-JennyNeural">Letter <say-as interpret-as="characters">S</say-as> makes <prosody rate="-28%" volume="x-loud"><phoneme alphabet="ipa" ph="s">sss</phoneme></prosody>.<break time="200ms"/> It's all air, no voice.<break time="200ms"/> Watch the screen - teeth almost together, tongue stays behind.<break time="300ms"/> Listen: <prosody rate="-25%" volume="loud"><phoneme alphabet="ipa" ph="s">sss</phoneme></prosody>.<break time="200ms"/> Say it: <prosody rate="-25%" volume="loud"><phoneme alphabet="ipa" ph="s">sss</phoneme></prosody>.</voice></speak>

visual.mouthCue: Teeth almost together, tongue stays behind; air flows out.

feedback.tts_exactMatch_ssml: <speak version="1.0" xml:lang="en-US" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2001/10/synthesis"><voice name="en-US-JennyNeural">Perfect.<break time="200ms"/> That's exactly <prosody rate="-25%" volume="loud"><phoneme alphabet="ipa" ph="s">sss</phoneme></prosody>.<break time="200ms"/> Locked in.</voice></speak>
feedback.tts_closeMatch: Almost - you're adding voice. It's just air: /sss/, not /zzz/. Watch the animation again.
feedback.tts_closeMatch_ssml: <speak version="1.0" xml:lang="en-US" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2001/10/synthesis"><voice name="en-US-JennyNeural">Almost - you're adding voice.<break time="200ms"/> It's just air: <prosody rate="-25%" volume="loud"><phoneme alphabet="ipa" ph="s">sss</phoneme></prosody>, not <prosody rate="-25%" volume="loud"><phoneme alphabet="ipa" ph="z">zzz</phoneme></prosody>.<break time="200ms"/> Watch the animation again.</voice></speak>
feedback.tts_wrongSound: That's not /sss/. Check the screen - teeth together, air only. Try again.
feedback.tts_wrongSound_ssml: <speak version="1.0" xml:lang="en-US" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2001/10/synthesis"><voice name="en-US-JennyNeural">That's not <prosody rate="-25%" volume="loud"><phoneme alphabet="ipa" ph="s">sss</phoneme></prosody>.<break time="200ms"/> Check the screen - teeth together, air only.<break time="200ms"/> Try again.</voice></speak>

blending_practice.tts_instruction: Now we put sounds together. Watch: /s/ /a/ /t/ to sat. Each sound connects smoothly. Your turn - blend it!
blending_practice.tts_instruction_ssml: <speak version="1.0" xml:lang="en-US" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2001/10/synthesis"><voice name="en-US-JennyNeural">Now we put sounds together.<break time="300ms"/> Watch:<break time="200ms"/> <prosody rate="-28%" volume="x-loud"><phoneme alphabet="ipa" ph="s">sss</phoneme></prosody> <prosody rate="-25%" volume="loud"><phoneme alphabet="x-sampa" ph="{">ah</phoneme></prosody> <prosody rate="-25%" volume="loud"><phoneme alphabet="ipa" ph="t">t</phoneme></prosody><break time="200ms"/> <say-as interpret-as="characters">s</say-as><break time="50ms"/><say-as interpret-as="characters">a</say-as><break time="50ms"/><say-as interpret-as="characters">t</say-as> to <emphasis>sat</emphasis>.<break time="300ms"/> Each sound connects smoothly.<break time="200ms"/> Your turn - blend it!</voice></speak>

feedback.tts_closeMatch: Not quite. Sound it out: /s/... /a/... /t/. Blend them.
feedback.tts_closeMatch_ssml: <speak version="1.0" xml:lang="en-US" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2001/10/synthesis"><voice name="en-US-JennyNeural">Not quite.<break time="200ms"/> Sound it out: <prosody rate="-25%" volume="loud"><phoneme alphabet="ipa" ph="s">sss</phoneme></prosody><break time="150ms"/> <prosody rate="-25%" volume="loud"><phoneme alphabet="x-sampa" ph="{">ah</phoneme></prosody><break time="150ms"/> <prosody rate="-25%" volume="loud"><phoneme alphabet="ipa" ph="t">t</phoneme></prosody>.<break time="200ms"/> Blend them.</voice></speak>
feedback.tts_wrongSound: Not quite. Sound it out: /s/... /a/... /t/. Blend them.
feedback.tts_wrongSound_ssml: <speak version="1.0" xml:lang="en-US" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2001/10/synthesis"><voice name="en-US-JennyNeural">Not quite.<break time="200ms"/> Sound it out: <prosody rate="-25%" volume="loud"><phoneme alphabet="ipa" ph="s">sss</phoneme></prosody><break time="150ms"/> <prosody rate="-25%" volume="loud"><phoneme alphabet="x-sampa" ph="{">ah</phoneme></prosody><break time="150ms"/> <prosody rate="-25%" volume="loud"><phoneme alphabet="ipa" ph="t">t</phoneme></prosody>.<break time="200ms"/> Blend them.</voice></speak>

introduction.tts_coach: Now let's check what stuck. You'll read words built from S, A, T, P, I, N. Aim for at least eight out of ten to hit mastery.
introduction.tts_coach_ssml: <speak version="1.0" xml:lang="en-US" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2001/10/synthesis"><voice name="en-US-JennyNeural">Now let's check what stuck.<break time="200ms"/> You'll read words built from <say-as interpret-as="characters">S</say-as>, <say-as interpret-as="characters">A</say-as>, <say-as interpret-as="characters">T</say-as>, <say-as interpret-as="characters">P</say-as>, <say-as interpret-as="characters">I</say-as>, and <say-as interpret-as="characters">N</say-as>.<break time="300ms"/> Aim for at least eight out of ten to hit mastery.</voice></speak>

feedback.tts_closeMatch: Not quite. Sound it out. Then blend.

results.tts_0-3_correct: Not quite there yet. We're going to break this down more. Sound-by-sound practice coming up.
results.tts_0-3_correct_ssml: <speak version="1.0" xml:lang="en-US" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2001/10/synthesis"><voice name="en-US-JennyNeural">Not quite there yet.<break time="200ms"/> We're going to break this down more.<break time="200ms"/> Sound-by-sound practice coming up.</voice></speak>
results.tts_4-7_correct: We're slowing down and fixing the sounds. Sound-by-sound practice coming up.
results.tts_4-7_correct_ssml: <speak version="1.0" xml:lang="en-US" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2001/10/synthesis"><voice name="en-US-JennyNeural">We're slowing down and fixing the sounds.<break time="200ms"/> Sound-by-sound practice coming up.</voice></speak>
results.tts_8-10_correct: You've got it. That's mastery - 8 out of 10 or better. Moving forward.
results.tts_8-10_correct_ssml: <speak version="1.0" xml:lang="en-US" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2001/10/synthesis"><voice name="en-US-JennyNeural">You've got it.<break time="200ms"/> That's mastery - eight out of ten or better.<break time="200ms"/> Moving forward.</voice></speak>

summary.tts_coach: Done. You learned six sounds: S, A, T, P, I, N. With just these six, you can read actual English words. That's not kids' stuff - that's functional literacy building. Next session, we add four more sounds, and your word count explodes. English has about 44 sounds total. You just learned 6. By the end of next lesson, you'll have 10. That's almost 25% of English phonics in two sessions. Stay sharp.
summary.tts_coach_ssml: <speak version="1.0" xml:lang="en-US" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2001/10/synthesis"><voice name="en-US-JennyNeural">Done.<break time="200ms"/> You learned six sounds: <say-as interpret-as="characters">S</say-as>, <say-as interpret-as="characters">A</say-as>, <say-as interpret-as="characters">T</say-as>, <say-as interpret-as="characters">P</say-as>, <say-as interpret-as="characters">I</say-as>, and <say-as interpret-as="characters">N</say-as>.<break time="300ms"/> With just these six, you can read actual English words.<break time="200ms"/> That's not kids' stuff - that's functional literacy building.<break time="400ms"/> Next session, we add four more sounds, and your word count explodes.<break time="300ms"/> English has about forty-four sounds total.<break time="200ms"/> You just learned six.<break time="300ms"/> By the end of next lesson, you'll have ten.<break time="200ms"/> That's almost twenty-five percent of English phonics in two sessions.<break time="300ms"/> Stay sharp.</voice></speak>
